local_names.japanese: ウォルナットブラウニー（2個）

aliases: ウォルナットブラウニー（2個）

japanese: ウォルナットブラウニー（2個）

region_of_origin: Japan (Western dessert served in a Japanese restaurant in Singapore, requiring Singaporean multilingual coverage)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general brownie entry; no walnut-specific brownie term exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Cakes, pastries and similar baked sweet products
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code specific to brownies or walnut brownies. Category-level code for baked sweet products used as closest parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code for brownie or walnut brownie found in clinical terminology. Not clinically coded.

serving.natural_language_portion: One serving is 2 pieces of walnut brownie, estimated at approximately 80 g total, as typically served at Sushi Express and similar Japanese casual dining outlets in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Brownies, prepared from recipe, with nuts — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB No. 18151)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for brownies prepared from recipe with nuts (NDB 18151) used as the primary nutrient reference. This is the closest approved-source match to a walnut brownie. Values are per 100 g edible portion. Serving size estimated at 80 g for 2 standard brownie pieces as sold at Sushi Express Singapore.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for walnut brownie found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 43 is derived from the published GI value for chocolate cake / fudge brownie analogues (GI ~40–45, low category) as reported in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283) and the University of Sydney GI Database. Walnuts have a negligible GI and do not materially raise the composite GI. GL per serving calculated as (43 × 45.6 g carbohydrate per 80 g serving) / 100 = 19.6. Carbohydrate per serving = 57.0 × 0.80 = 45.6 g.

hypertension.guidance: Each 2-piece serving provides approximately 140 mg sodium; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and avoid pairing with other high-sodium foods in the same meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar and refined carbohydrate content with moderate glycemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: With approximately 30 g of sugar and a glycemic load of ~19.6 per 2-piece serving, walnut brownies should be consumed only occasionally and in small portions by individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, ideally as part of a mixed meal to blunt the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat, cholesterol, and added sugar
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Each serving contains approximately 4 g saturated fat and 50 mg cholesterol; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption and note that walnuts provide beneficial polyunsaturated omega-3 fatty acids (ALA) which partially offset the saturated fat burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Walnut Brownie (2 pieces) is a baked chocolate dessert consisting of dense, fudgy chocolate brownie squares studded with chopped walnuts. The brownie is made from a batter of wheat flour, cocoa powder or melted chocolate, sugar, butter, and eggs, baked until set with a slightly crackled top and a moist, chewy interior. Walnuts add a crunchy texture contrast and a mild bitterness that complements the rich chocolate flavour. This item is served as a dessert at Sushi Express, a Japanese casual dining chain in Singapore, where it appears alongside sushi and bento offerings as a Western-style sweet treat. Nutritionally, a 2-piece serving (~80 g) provides approximately 332 kcal, 45.6 g carbohydrate, 30 g sugar, 15.2 g fat, and 3.8 g protein. The glycemic index is estimated at 43 (low), but the glycemic load per serving is approximately 19.6, which is moderate-to-high, making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. Walnuts contribute polyunsaturated fatty acids including alpha-linolenic acid (ALA), an omega-3 precursor. The item contains gluten, eggs, dairy, and tree nuts (walnuts) and is not suitable for individuals with these allergens. It is vegetarian but not vegan or halal-certified.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a walnut brownie?
Is walnut brownie suitable for diabetics?
What is the GI of a chocolate brownie?

confidence_reason: No exact nutrient data for this specific commercial product (Sushi Express Walnut Brownie) was found in any approved primary source. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy entry for brownies prepared from recipe with nuts (NDB 18151), which is the closest approved-source analogue. GI is estimated from published analogous brownie/chocolate cake values in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entry for this food category. All other numeric fields are reported from USDA SR Legacy. Serving size of 80 g for 2 pieces is an estimate based on typical commercial brownie portion sizes; the actual weight per piece at Sushi Express Singapore is not publicly documented in an approved source. The cuisine tag 'Japanese' reflects the restaurant context (Sushi Express) rather than the dish's culinary origin, which is Western/American. GI of 43 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for walnut brownie was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GL per serving = (43 × 45.6) / 100 = 19.6. Halal status is not confirmed for this product. The region_of_origin clarifies the dual context of a Western dessert served in a Japanese restaurant in Singapore, which necessitates compliance with Singaporean multilingual naming requirements (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) in addition to Japanese.
